title: Second-Order FALQON Parameter Transfer for the Max-Cut Problem on 3-Regular Graphs
authors: Gabriel Fernandes Thomaz, Eduarda Rodrigues Monteiro, Jerusa Marchi, Marcelo Zen Pretto, Alisson dos Passos Fumaco, Evandro Chagas Ribeiro da Rosa
abstract: The Feedback-based Algorithm for Quantum Optimization (FALQON) offers a deterministic alternative to variational quantum algorithms by bypassing classical optimization loops. However, maintaining convergence on large problem instances often requires restricting the time step, necessitating quantum circuit depths that exceed Noisy Intermediate-Scale Quantum (NISQ) hardware capabilities. This paper investigates the parameter transferability of second-order FALQON applied to the Max-Cut problem on 3-regular graphs. Through numerical experiments evaluating quantum circuits up to 16 layers on graphs up to 24 nodes, we demonstrate a highly advantageous scaling behavior: transferring feedback parameters optimized on small instances to larger target graphs yields significantly higher approximation ratios than natively optimizing the parameters directly on the larger graphs. This performance advantage arises because parameters trained on smaller instances can safely adopt aggressively larger time steps. By offloading the expensive parameter discovery phase to small-scale instances, this transfer strategy simultaneously reduces computational overhead and enhances the approximation ratio, thereby bringing FALQON closer to practical viability on near-term quantum architectures.
summary: This paper demonstrates that parameters for the FALQON quantum optimization algorithm can be effectively transferred from small graphs to larger ones. This strategy avoids the need for deep circuits on large instances by using 'aggressive' time steps learned from small-scale simulations, significantly improving the approximation ratio for the Max-Cut problem.
main_problem: The FALQON algorithm faces a scalability bottleneck where increasing problem size requires smaller time steps to ensure convergence, leading to prohibitively deep quantum circuits.
main_result: Transferring second-order FALQON parameters (time steps and feedback coefficients) from small graphs to larger ones yields significantly higher approximation ratios than native optimization on the larger graphs.
method: The authors use a parameter transfer strategy where feedback parameters are optimized on small-scale 3-regular graphs and applied to larger target graphs, utilizing a second-order feedback rule.
model_or_system: The study focuses on the Max-Cut problem on 3-regular graphs, using a cost Hamiltonian based on Pauli-Z operators and a mixing Hamiltonian based on Pauli-X operators.
key_observables: Approximation ratio (the ratio of the final expectation value to the exact ground state energy).
important_parameters: Time step (delta t), feedback parameters (beta_k), circuit depth (number of layers), and graph size (number of nodes n).
main_assumptions: The success of the transfer assumes local structural similarities between graphs and is tested on 3-regular graphs with uniform local structure.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the power-law decay of the optimal time step as a function of graph size; Figure 2 is a heatmap showing the average approximation ratio achieved via parameter transfer.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the FALQON scalability problem, describes the second-order algorithm and the parameter transfer strategy, presents numerical experiments on 3-regular graphs, and discusses the scaling advantages and future directions.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1. Average optimal time step (∆t) that maximizes the approximation ratio after 16 layers, plotted on a log-log scale as a function of the 3-regular graph size (n). The monotonically decreasing trend exhibits a strict power-law decay with a fitted regression of ∆t ≈0.4984·n−0.5110, indicating that the time step scales approximately as 1/(2√n).

Fig. 2. FALQON parameter transferability matrix. The heatmap displays the average approximation ratio achieved when evaluating 16-layer FALQON circuits using parameters optimized on training graphs (ntrain, y-axis) applied to the Hamiltonians of target graphs (ntarget, x-axis).

Fig. 3. Approximation ratio of natively optimized parameters (solid red line) versus parameters transferred from smaller instances (dashed lines). The native performance decays significantly as the target graph size (n) increases due to the algorithm selecting progressively smaller time steps (∆t) that restrict exploration within the fixed 16-layer limit. In contrast, transferring parameters from smaller graphs (particularly n = 6) significantly outperforms native optimization on large graphs.
